title: Writing - Part 1: Electric Vehicles & Green Transport
description: Your class has listened to a discussion on electric vehicles and green transport, exploring the opportunities and obstacles involved in transitioning away from fossil-fuel-powered transportation.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the challenges in your notes. You should explain which challenge you consider most critical to overcome for a successful green transport transition, giving reasons for your opinion.

challenges: The inadequacy of public charging infrastructure, particularly in rural and lower-income areas
The high purchase price of electric vehicles relative to conventional petrol and diesel cars
Concerns about the environmental impact of battery production and end-of-life disposal
opinions: Patchy charging infrastructure makes electric car ownership impractical for many.
High upfront costs put electric vehicles out of reach for most.
Battery production raises serious environmental and human rights concerns.

structure.introduction: Introduce the electric vehicle transition and its importance for decarbonising transport, identifying the two challenges your essay will examine.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Analyse the first challenge, drawing on specific examples to illustrate its scope and impact on adoption rates.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Examine the second challenge and argue why it represents the most critical barrier to be overcome.
structure.conclusion: Summarise your argument and offer a brief reflection on what a genuinely equitable and sustainable EV transition would require.

content.modelAnswer:
The transition to electric vehicles has emerged as a central pillar of transport decarbonisation policy in many countries, yet the pace of adoption has been uneven, and the barriers preventing wider uptake remain substantial. Among the most significant are the inadequacy of public charging infrastructure and the high purchase price of electric vehicles relative to conventional alternatives. Both deserve serious policy attention, though they are not of equal urgency.

The shortfalls in charging infrastructure create practical barriers that directly affect consumers' willingness to switch. In rural areas and urban streets without private driveways, access to reliable charging is inconsistent and, in some regions, severely limited. Unlike refuelling a petrol vehicle — a process that takes minutes at almost any high street location — EV charging requires either home installation, access to workplace facilities or confidence that public chargers will be available and operational. Where this confidence cannot be established, the decision to purchase an electric vehicle becomes difficult to justify, regardless of environmental motivation.

Nevertheless, I would argue that the affordability barrier constitutes the most critical challenge, precisely because it determines who participates in the transition at all. If electric vehicles remain accessible primarily to higher-income households, the environmental benefits of decarbonising transport will be unevenly distributed, while lower-income drivers continue to operate older, more polluting vehicles. Purchase subsidies, salary sacrifice schemes and competitive second-hand EV markets are all mechanisms that could broaden access — but only if deliberately prioritised.

In conclusion, a green transport transition that fails to address affordability will be neither equitable nor, ultimately, transformative in scale.
